era_context: The year of Alexander the Great's death in Babylon (June 323 BCE) and the immediate aftermath. This marks the end of the Hellenistic conquest period and the beginning of the Wars of the Diadochi (succession wars). The Mauryan Empire continues consolidating power in India. Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Levant are transitioning from unified Macedonian rule to fragmented Hellenistic kingdoms. Greece itself is experiencing the Lamian War as city-states attempt to reassert independence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources are sparse for this specific year)

events.title: Death of Alexander the Great in Babylon
events.description: Alexander III of Macedon dies in Babylon on June 10 or 11, 323 BCE, at approximately 32 years of age. The cause of death is uncertain—ancient sources cite fever, poisoning, or complications from a wound. His death triggers the immediate fragmentation of his empire and the beginning of the Wars of the Diadochi. Alexander's body is claimed by Ptolemy I and transported to Egypt, becoming a symbol of legitimacy for the Ptolemaic dynasty. The death of Alexander marks the end of the Hellenistic conquest period and the beginning of the Hellenistic kingdoms period.
key_figures: Roxana (wife of Alexander, pregnant at his death)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's death is one of the most securely dated events in ancient history. The date of June 10 or 11, 323 BCE is accepted by modern scholarship, though the exact cause of death remains debated.

events.title: Partition of Alexander's empire among the Diadochi (Succession Wars begin)
events.description: Following Alexander's death, his generals (the Diadochi) meet in Babylon to partition his empire. Perdiccas is appointed regent for Alexander's heirs (his half-brother Philip Arrhidaeus and his unborn son Alexander IV). The satrapies are distributed: Ptolemy receives Egypt, Seleucus receives Babylon, Antigonus receives Phrygia, Lysimachus receives Thrace, and others receive various territories. This partition is unstable and leads to decades of warfare as the Diadochi compete for supremacy. The Wars of the Diadochi (323–281 BCE) will ultimately result in the establishment of the three major Hellenistic kingdoms: Ptolemaic Egypt, Seleucid Syria, and Antigonid Macedon.
events.certainty_note: The partition of Alexander's empire is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure within ±1 year.
cross_references: -281-001 (Battle of Corupedium, final resolution of Diadochi wars)

events.title: Lamian War: Greek city-states revolt against Macedonian rule
events.description: Following Alexander's death, the Greek city-states, led by Athens and Aetolia, attempt to throw off Macedonian control in the Lamian War (also called the Hellenic War). The revolt is triggered by the death of Alexander and the perceived weakness of Macedonian authority under the regency of Perdiccas and Antipater. The war lasts approximately one year and ends with the defeat of the Greek forces at the Battle of Crannon (322 BCE) and the reassertion of Macedonian control. The war marks the end of Greek city-state independence and the beginning of Macedonian hegemony over Greece.
key_figures: Leosthenes (Athenian general, leader of the revolt)
Demosthenes (Athenian orator, supporter of the revolt)
events.certainty_note: The Lamian War is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 323–322 BCE is secure.
cross_references: -322-001 (Battle of Crannon ends the Lamian War)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya defeats the Nanda dynasty; Mauryan Empire expands
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, with the support of his advisor Kautilya, defeats the last Nanda emperor and consolidates control over Magadha and surrounding territories. The Mauryan Empire begins its rapid expansion across northern India. The exact chronology is debated, with some sources placing this event in 322 or 321 BCE, but 323 BCE is also defensible. This marks the beginning of the Mauryan Empire's rise to dominance in the Indian subcontinent.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's rise is uncertain. Indian sources and Greek sources give conflicting dates. Modern scholarship places the fall of the Nanda dynasty between 322–320 BCE. The exact year is debated, with 323 BCE being one of several defensible dates.

events.title: Ptolemy I establishes Alexandria as capital of Egypt; begins Ptolemaic dynasty
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter, having secured Egypt as his satrapy following Alexander's death, establishes Alexandria as the capital of Egypt and the center of Ptolemaic rule. He moves Alexander's body to Alexandria, using it as a symbol of legitimacy and continuity. Alexandria becomes a major Hellenistic center of learning, trade, and culture. This marks the beginning of the Ptolemaic dynasty, which will rule Egypt for nearly three centuries until the death of Cleopatra VII in 30 BCE.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's establishment of Alexandria as capital is well-attested. The date is secure within ±1 year.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Alexander's death is traditionally given as June 10 or 11, 323 BCE, but some ancient sources give different dates (e.g., some sources suggest June 13). The cause of death remains debated—ancient sources cite fever, poisoning, or complications from a wound, but modern medical historians have proposed various diagnoses (malaria, typhoid, acute pancreatitis, etc.). The chronology of Chandragupta's rise is highly uncertain; some sources place the fall of the Nanda dynasty in 322 BCE or 321 BCE rather than 323 BCE. The significance of the Lamian War has been debated by modern scholars; some argue it was less significant than traditionally portrayed and that Greek city-states retained more autonomy than previously thought. The partition of Alexander's empire was not as clean or final as traditionally depicted; the satrapies were redistributed multiple times in the following years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 323 BCE is rich in Greek sources but heavily influenced by the perspectives of the Diadochi and their successors. The sources are often written decades or centuries after the events. Indian sources on Chandragupta are even more distant from the events they describe and are influenced by religious and dynastic narratives. The synchronization between Greek and Indian chronologies remains problematic. Archaeological evidence for this year is sparse. Western scholarship has traditionally privileged Greek sources and the Hellenistic world, potentially underrepresenting Indian and Persian perspectives. The exact causes and consequences of events in 323 BCE are often reconstructed through inference rather than direct evidence.

graph_edges.note: Alexander's death triggers the partition of his empire
Alexander's death triggers the Lamian War in Greece
The unstable partition leads to the First War of the Diadochi
The Lamian War continues and ends with the Battle of Crannon in 322 BCE
Chandragupta's consolidation of power leads to his later encounter with Seleucus I
Ptolemy's establishment of Alexandria leads to his declaration as king in 305 BCE
